column17: Colonic polyps are focal mucosal masses protruding into the lumen. CT colonography and air/double-contrast barium enema show smooth intraluminal nodules that may be sessile, pedunculated, or flat; classic barium enema appearances include Mexican-hat and bowler-hat configurations, and carpet lesions can be subtle. CT colonography relies on bowel prep and stool tagging plus supine/prone acquisitions to distinguish stool from true polyps. Polyps ≤5 mm are generally not reported, while polyps ≥1 cm are referred for colonoscopic polypectomy. Key mimics include retained fecal debris, diverticula/inverted diverticulum, intramural masses (e.g., lipoma), and carcinoma; C-RADS standardizes reporting with follow-up recommendations.

column5: Air-contrast barium enema. Based on the image, name 3 key differentials and give 1 best discriminator for each.
column6: Retained fecal debris; Colonic diverticulosis / inverted diverticulum; Intramural mass (lipoma); Colon carcinoma
column7: Retained fecal debris: should be mobile and on the dependent surface; often irregular with internal contrast or gas; bowel prep/tagging and positional comparisons help avoid confusion.

Diverticulosis / inverted diverticulum: can simulate a polyp on BE en face; patient rotation/profile view helps show an outpouching rather than a protruding mucosal mass; CT colonography typically clarifies.
Lipoma: CT is diagnostic by fat density.
Carcinoma: any polyp can contain invasive carcinoma; definitive distinction requires colonoscopy/biopsy.

column15: For Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), what is the typical presentation (classic features)?
column16: Autosomal-dominant syndrome with innumerable premalignant adenomatous colon polyps and ~100% colon cancer risk; prophylactic colectomy is standard prevention.

column15: Named syndrome: innumerable premalignant adenomatous colon polyps + ~100% colon cancer risk → what is the syndrome?
For Gardner syndrome, what is the typical presentation (classic features)?
column16: FAP variant: colon polyps plus desmoid tumors, osteomas, papillary thyroid cancer, epidermoid cysts.

column15: Named syndrome: FAP + desmoids + osteomas + papillary thyroid cancer + epidermoid cysts → what is the syndrome?
For Turcot syndrome, what is the typical presentation (classic features)?
column16: FAP variant: colon polyps plus CNS tumors (gliomas and medulloblastomas).

column15: Named syndrome: FAP + CNS tumors (gliomas/medulloblastomas) → what is the syndrome?
For Lynch syndrome (HNPCC), what is the typical presentation (classic features)?
column16: Autosomal-dominant syndrome due to DNA mismatch repair causing microsatellite instability colon cancer; associated cancers include endometrial, gastric, small bowel, liver, and biliary malignancies.

column15: Named syndrome: DNA mismatch repair → microsatellite instability colon cancer + associated endometrial cancer → what is the syndrome?
For Cronkhite–Canada, what is the typical presentation (classic features)?
column16: Non-inherited hamartomatous polyposis plus abnormal skin pigmentation, alopecia, and nail dystrophy (onychodystrophy).

column15: Named syndrome: hamartomatous GI polyps + skin pigmentation changes + alopecia + nail dystrophy → what is the syndrome?
On CT colonography, what polyp size is generally not reported?
column16: Polyps ≤ 5 mm are generally not reported.

column15: In C-RADS colorectal findings, what are the recommended follow-up intervals for C1 vs C2, and what is recommended for C3/C4?
column16: C1: repeat in 5–10 years. C2: surveillance within 3 years. C3/C4: consider colonoscopy/biopsy.
